author: @stevetobias4890
text: Being an amputee, I have been asked to look into "Naked Prosthetic Finger Drivers" I have only my left thumb, which is normal. I have lived almost 40 years as an amputee,  my first job was a bricklayers assistant because I have been told all my life what I can or can not do. I am the judge and jury of my abilities, not another person who has not lived my life experience. Give up, I know what I can or can not do.

author: @russellzauner
text:
We haven't needed implants for a long time.  I've held in my hands and used devices as well as torn them apart and worked on a team at Intel in 2013 that built software for them (as well as firm ware in some projects) such as the Myo and Mindflex (by Mattel!) among others I can't disclose because it was our hardware - but it's pretty much sensor harnesses and field detection now, local and remote (yes, reading brain waves at a distance).
If we can take oxygen/glucose readings in the blood without sticking a needle, we should be able to capture some electrical activity without jamming probes into our brains.

author: @ogi22
text:
This is simply amazing. When I was a kid, we had a tube TV, black and white. And we were sometimes going to our grandparents in the evenings, just to watch a kid show for goodnight on their colour TV. I have seen cassette recorders and walkmens. Then CD and DVD players. Now using USB and micro cards. I saw my dad having fun with ZX Spectrum computer, trying to install a program from a cassette. Then we did the same on my friend's Atari, having fun with games. I played with 286, 382, 486, Pentiums... And now, JDWS shows images of an early universe. Physics is starting to poke quantum gravity for real. We measure gravity waves and space stretching. Weak AI starts to take shape. People experiment with direct human-machine interface. Orwell is no longer a fantasy. Getting closer to Asimov and Lem...
May you live in interesting times they said. So we do now 😁
I wonder what will be in a few years. Cyberpunk welcome? Johnny Mnemonic? 😜

author: @pendarischneider
text:
There is a tweet, repeated elsewhere, from @daisyowl relevant to this talk --
"if you ever code something that 'feels like a hack but it works,' just remember that a CPU is literally a rock that we tricked into thinking"
"not to oversimplify: first you have to flatten the rock and put lightning inside it"

author: @kenrowe167
text: Colossus used around 2400 vacuum tubes and thyratrons, not electro-mechanical relays. It is the first electronic computer, not Eniac.

replies.author: @AnonymousAnarchist2
replies.text:
yes. And was classified as a top national secrect for a very very long time.

Likewise we are starting to see the USA declassify documents regarding a pure magentetic logic system from the 1940's and 1950's that seem to refference an field protable computer, but no computer is known.

author: @JosephEvans-r6w
text:
Just for historical accuracy 
The electromechanical series of machines at Bletchley park were mostly varieties/ revisions of a device called called "Bombe" named after the polish machine of the same name, then there was a hybrid generation called the "heath Robinson" machines that used a both electromechanical and thermionic components then the "Colussus" series of machines were designed to use thermionic Valves (about 1600 in the mark 1)" colossus was about 1943 Eniac was around 1945.

author: @McRootbeer
text: Hey Rob, do you think these liquid electrolytes could be used to make a high speed shunt that could be used to protect sensitive electronics? With all the talk of war going on, EMPs keep coming up as a big threat. Besides faraday cages, can you think of any EMP protections the home builder can make?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is an interesting idea mate but to be honest a faraday cage is really going to be the best i think

replies.author: @McRootbeer
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Haven't you always said there is no "best" solution, only the most appropriate for a given circumstance?  It's not feasible to put a faraday cage over a car, or something plugged into the grid.  From what I've read other EMP protections are about capturing excess voltage spikes with circuits or magnetic materials.  Products that do that seem expensive, $70 is the lowest I've seen, and the price rises to over $100 for some. Am I misunderstanding or is this in its simplest form something magnetic, but insulated, put around a power cord or battery terminal? And can it feasibly be made by an average person at home?

author: @robertmoore8166
text: No direct connection to the brain is needed. Lookup "Alterego" from M.I.T. It senses the neural muscular impulses that the brain generates when you think of words in your brain. You can think sentences and directly control a computer just by thinking.
